metadata.title: Revisiting FRB 20121102A: milliarcsecond localisation and a decreasing dispersion measure
metadata.abstract:
FRB 20121102A is the original repeating fast radio burst (FRB) source and also the first to be localised to milliarcsecond precision using very-long-baseline interferometry (VLBI). It has been active for over 13 years and resides in an extreme magneto-ionic environment in a dwarf host galaxy at a distance of ∼1 Gpc. In this work, we use the European VLBI Network (EVN) to (re-)localise FRB 20121102A and its associated persistent radio source (PRS). We confirm that the two are co-located — improving on previous results by a factor of ∼4 and constraining the FRB and PRS co-location to ∼12 pc transverse offset. Over a decade, the PRS luminosity on milliarcsecond scales remains consistent with measurements on larger angular scales, showing that the PRS is still compact. We also present the detection of 18 bursts with the Nançay Radio Telescope (NRT) as part of our ÉCLAT monitoring program. These bursts, together with previously published results, show that the observed dispersion measure (DM) of FRB 20121102A has dropped by ∼25 pc cm[−][3] in the past five years, highlighting a fractional decrease in the local DM contribution of ≳15 %. We discuss potential physical scenarios and highlight possible future observations that will help reveal the nature of FRB 20121102A, which is one of only a few known FRBs with a luminous PRS. 

**Key words.** fast radio bursts – persistent radio sources – very long baseline interferometry – astrometry

metadata.introduction:
Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are millisecond-duration, extragalactic flashes of coherent radio waves whose origin(s) and emission mechanism(s) are still poorly understood (see, e.g., Petroff et al. 2022, for a review). A small fraction (∼3 %; e.g., Spitler et al. 2016; CHIME/FRB Collaboration et al. 2023) of the known FRB population (≳3, 000 sources; e.g., Wang et al. 2025a) has been observed to repeat, implying that these sources have longerlived central engines that are capable of producing repeat bursts on timescales from minutes to several years. It remains unclear if all FRBs repeat and have the same progenitors (see Pleunis et al. 2021 and also, e.g., Ould-Boukattine et al. 2024; CHIME/FRB Collaboration et al. 2025, and references therein). 

In this work, we focus on FRB 20121102A, the first known repeater (Spitler et al. 2016), and one of the best-studied FRB sources. FRB 20121102A was precisely localised, using very-long-baseline interferometry (VLBI; Chatterjee et al. 2017; Marcote et al. 2017), to a low-metallicity, star-forming dwarf galaxy at a redshift of _z_ ∼ 0.193 (Tendulkar et al. 2017; Bassa et al. 2017). At a position that is consistent with the bursts, a persistent radio source (PRS) was found (Chatterjee et al. 2017); this source remains compact in inter-continental VLBI observations with the European VLBI Network (EVN) and has a luminosity of ∼5 × 10[38] erg s[−][1] (Marcote et al. 2017). The transverse size of the PRS is ≲0.7 pc (diameter) and the projected separation between the burst source and the PRS is ≲40 pc (Marcote et al. 2017). FRB 20121102A’s co-location with this luminous PRS makes it a peculiar source compared to the vast majority of the FRB population, with the closest-known analogue being FRB 20190520B (Niu et al. 2022; Bhandari et al. 2023). Whether these sources represent an evolutionary stage common to repeaters in general, or instead a distinct subclass, is unclear. The PRS provides important insight into the nature of FRB 20121102A and its local environment — as we discuss in detail in Section 5, where we consider four possible PRS origins: 1) a nebula powered by a magnetar; 2) an accreting binary; 3) a magnetically interacting binary; and 4) a ‘wandering’ massive black hole. 

FRB 20121102A is unreliably detectable, making follow-up and monitoring observations challenging. Its burst rate changes on timescales of hours to years and its activity may be periodic at 159.3 days — albeit with a >50 % duty cycle (see, e.g., Braga et al. 2025, and references therein). 

Nonetheless, monitoring of propagation effects incurred by the bursts also provides valuable insights. For example, both the dispersion measure (DM) and Faraday rotation measure (RM) of the FRB 20121102A bursts have varied wildly in the past decade. The DM first increased by ∼6 pc cm[−][3] before dropping by ∼20 pc cm[−][3] (Wang et al. 2025b). These changes must be in the local environment, where DMhost+local is estimated to be < 225 pc cm[−][3] (Tendulkar et al. 2017) and depends on how much DM should be ascribed to the interstellar medium (ISM) of the host. Thus, the fractional change in DMhost+local must be large, ≳ 10 %. This behaviour is not clearly seen in other repeating FRBs. 

FRB 20121102A also shows an exceptionally large and highly variable RM[1] , which was ∼181 × 10[3] rad m[−][2] in 2016, and by 2023 dropped to ∼44 × 10[3] rad m[−][2] (Wang et al. 2025b; Michilli et al. 2018; Plavin et al. 2022; Hilmarsson et al. 2021). Even after the RM decreased by ∼75 %, it is still higher than the 

> 1 Each RM value is quoted in the corresponding source rest frame. To convert it to the observer frame simply divide the value by (1 + _z_ )[2] , where _z_ is the redshift of the host galaxy of FRB 20121102A. 

next-highest |RM| FRB (FRB 20190520B; Anna-Thomas et al. 2023) and several orders of magnitude larger than typical FRB RMs (e.g., Pandhi et al. 2024). The high RM, which is likely caused by the local environment of the source, combined with its variability and the apparently associated PRS, together indicate that FRB 20121102A resides in an extreme and dynamic magneto-ionic environment (Michilli et al. 2018). 

Associations between (repeating) FRB sources and PRSs remain rare. Besides FRB 20121102A, there is the previously mentioned FRB 20190520B. Like FRB 20121102A, this source is also localised to a dwarf galaxy and shows large RM variations, including sign flips (Niu et al. 2022; Anna-Thomas et al. 2023; Bhandari et al. 2023). Recently, it was confirmed that FRB 20190417A is also associated with a PRS (Ibik et al. 2024; Moroianu et al. 2025). The host of this source is also a dwarf galaxy and the bursts show large RM variations of ∼10[3] rad m[−][2] month[−][1] (∼20 % fractional change). Other proposed FRB-PRS systems associated with FRB 20201124A and FRB 20240114A are much less luminous and with much lower RMs (Bruni et al. 2024, 2025). 

Continued monitoring of FRB 20121102A and its nearby PRS are key to deciphering the physical nature of this system. While much has been gleaned from the observations to date, there are important missing pieces. Marcote et al. (2017) showed that the associated PRS is compact on sub-milliarcsecond scales; however, due to limited ( _u_ , _v_ )-coverage in their VLBI observations they were only able to constrain the physical offset between the burst source and PRS to <40 pc — leaving open the possibility that the two sources might be slightly offset from each other. Likewise, while several telescopes have monitored the flux density of the PRS (e.g., Rhodes et al. 2023; Bhardwaj et al. 2025), there are few VLBI observations to verify whether the flux density tracks in the same way on milliarcsecond scales, and whether a substantial fraction of the flux density resolves out on long baselines. 

Motivated to robustly establish as much as possible about the PRS, we observed FRB 20121102A with the EVN, triggered by a report of enhanced burst activity (Wang et al. 2022). In this paper, we present the results of our EVN campaign towards FRB 20121102A, and place a strong constraint on the colocation of the burst source and PRS. We also present the results of our FRB 20121102A monitoring campaign with the Nançay Radio Telescope (NRT). We discuss possible scenarios that explain the FRB and PRS association, combined with the evolving DM and RM of the source.

It has been active for over 13 years and resides in an extreme magneto-ionic environment in a dwarf host galaxy at a distance of ∼1 Gpc. In this work, we use the European VLBI Network (EVN) to (re-)localise FRB 20121102A and its associated persistent radio source (PRS). We confirm that the two are co-located — improving on previous results by a factor of ∼4 and constraining the FRB and PRS co-location to ∼12 pc transverse offset. Over a decade, the PRS luminosity on milliarcsecond scales remains consistent with measurements on larger angular scales, showing that the PRS is still compact. We also present the detection of 18 bursts with the Nançay Radio Telescope (NRT) as part of our ÉCLAT monitoring program. These bursts, together with previously published results, show that the observed dispersion measure (DM) of FRB 20121102A has dropped by ∼25 pc cm[−][3] in the past five years, highlighting a fractional decrease in the local DM contribution of ≳15 %. We discuss potential physical scenarios and highlight possible future observations that will help reveal the nature of FRB 20121102A, which is one of only a few known FRBs with a luminous PRS.

FRB 20121102A also shows an exceptionally large and highly variable RM[1] , which was ∼181 × 10[3] rad m[−][2] in 2016, and by 2023 dropped to ∼44 × 10[3] rad m[−][2] (Wang et al. 2025b; Michilli et al. 2018; Plavin et al. 2022; Hilmarsson et al. 2021). Even after the RM decreased by ∼75 %, it is still higher than the next-highest |RM| FRB (FRB 20190520B; Anna-Thomas et al. 2023) and several orders of magnitude larger than typical FRB RMs (e.g., Pandhi et al. 2024). The high RM, which is likely caused by the local environment of the source, combined with its variability and the apparently associated PRS, together indicate that FRB 20121102A resides in an extreme and dynamic magneto-ionic environment (Michilli et al. 2018).

1) _Isolated magnetar_ : In this scenario, the FRB 20121102A burst source is an isolated magnetar and the PRS is a rotation-powered pulsar wind nebula (PWN) or magnetically powered magnetar wind nebula (MWN; Margalit et al. 2018; Rahaman et al. 2025). Alternatively, or in addition to the PWN/MWN scenario, the PRS could also represent the remnant from a super-luminous supernova (SLSN) or a long gamma-ray burst (LGRB) that is interacting with the circumstellar medium. The bursts are created by explosive magnetic reconnection events (Lyubarsky 2020), curvature radiation (Kumar et al. 2017), a synchrotron maser (e.g., Lyubarsky 2014; Beloborodov 2017; Metzger et al. 2019), or some other emission mechanism (e.g., Thompson 2019; Long & Pe’er 2023). The PRS is powered by sporadic magnetar flares and/or could result from magnetic braking of a magnetar that was born spinning rapidly (period of tens of milliseconds or less) and quickly spun-down, dumping its kinetic energy into its surroundings. The closest-known Galactic analogues would be SGR 1935+2154, which created much weaker but still FRB-like bursts (Bochenek et al. 2020; CHIME/FRB Collaboration et al. 2020; Kirsten et al. 2021); the Crab Nebula, though it is ∼10[4] –10[5] × less luminous than the FRB 20121102A PRS (e.g., De Looze et al. 2019, and references therein); and Swift J1834.9−0846, the only Galactic magnetar with a known nebula (Younes et al. 2016).

1) _Isolated magnetar_ : In this scenario, the FRB 20121102A burst source is an isolated magnetar and the PRS is a rotation-powered pulsar wind nebula (PWN) or magnetically powered magnetar wind nebula (MWN; Margalit et al. 2018; Rahaman et al. 2025). Alternatively, or in addition to the PWN/MWN scenario, the PRS could also represent the remnant from a super-luminous supernova (SLSN) or a long gamma-ray burst (LGRB) that is interacting with the circumstellar medium. The bursts are created by explosive magnetic reconnection events (Lyubarsky 2020), curvature radiation (Kumar et al. 2017), a synchrotron maser (e.g., Lyubarsky 2014; Beloborodov 2017; Metzger et al. 2019), or some other emission mechanism (e.g., Thompson 2019; Long & Pe’er 2023). The PRS is powered by sporadic magnetar flares and/or could result from magnetic braking of a magnetar that was born spinning rapidly (period of tens of milliseconds or less) and quickly spun-down, dumping its kinetic energy into its surroundings. The closest-known Galactic analogues would be SGR 1935+2154, which created much weaker but still FRB-like bursts (Bochenek et al. 2020; CHIME/FRB Collaboration et al. 2020; Kirsten et al. 2021); the Crab Nebula, though it is ∼10[4] –10[5] × less luminous than the FRB 20121102A PRS (e.g., De Looze et al. 2019, and references therein); and Swift J1834.9−0846, the only Galactic magnetar with a known nebula (Younes et al. 2016).

2) _Accreting binary_ : In this scenario, FRB 20121102A is a black hole (BH) or neutron star (NS) that is accreting at hyper-Eddington rates from a binary stellar companion and launching a relativistic jet into the circum-binary medium. The PRS would be the synchrotron radiation from electrons energized at the disk wind/jet termination shock (Sridhar & Metzger 2022; Sridhar et al. 2024), and the bursts would be generated via reconnection of striped magnetic fields inside the jet or due to magnetized forward shocks harbouring synchrotron maser instability (Sridhar et al. 2021). In this scenario, the bursts are beamed along the jet’s narrow funnel. The jetted Galactic microquasar SS 433 and its SNR W 50 nebula provide a potential analogue, though the accretion rate of this system is uncertain; furthermore, the precessing and flaring jet associated with SS 433 is not aligned to our line of sight, which would prevent the detection of any FRBs. It is thought that SS 433 could be an off-axis Galactic equivalent of ultra-luminous X-ray sources (ULXs). Extragalactic ULXs provide an example of hyper-Eddington accretors; however, none are known to have produced an FRB-like burst, though potentially due to a lack of concerted search for ms-duration flares from known highly-accreting ‘microblazars’ — i.e., stellar-mass, accreting compact objects with a jet pointed at us.

3) _Interacting binary_ : An alternative binary scenario involves magnetic interaction and/or wind interaction between two closely orbiting bodies, one of which could be a magnetar or at least a high-B-field neutron star (Zhang 2017; Mottez et al. 2020). The recent discovery that some long-period transients are magnetically interacting white dwarf – M-dwarf binaries (de Ruiter et al. 2025) provides a potential analogy, though the mechanism would need to scale-up enormously in energy to explain FRB 20121102A’s bursts. In this scenario, the radio bursts could be from particles accelerated along a magnetic bridge connecting the two bodies; the origin of the PRS would be from an intra-binary shock between the stellar winds (e.g., Marcote et al. 2016, and references therein).

4) _Indirect association_ : Though the FRB 20121102A burst source and the PRS are extremely close to each other, it remains possible that they are not physically associated, at least not in a direct sense. In this scenario, the PRS could be a ‘wandering’ or ‘off-nuclear’ massive accreting BH (MBH; Reines et al. 2020). Low-Eddington accretion onto an MBH, with a mass of 10[4] –10[7] M⊙, would power a (near-)constant luminosity PRS through small-scale jets. The source of the FRBs would be a compact object, most likely a magnetar, that resides within 0.1–10 pc of the MBH and happens to be there because the MBH is embedded in a star cluster (Askar et al. 2023). An analogue would be the Galactic Centre magnetar SGR J1745−2900, which is situated ∼0.1 pc from Sgr A[∗] (Desvignes et al. 2018).

The closest-known Galactic analogues would be SGR 1935+2154, which created much weaker but still FRB-like bursts (Bochenek et al. 2020; CHIME/FRB Collaboration et al. 2020; Kirsten et al. 2021); the Crab Nebula, though it is ∼10[4] –10[5] × less luminous than the FRB 20121102A PRS (e.g., De Looze et al. 2019, and references therein); and Swift J1834.9−0846, the only Galactic magnetar with a known nebula (Younes et al. 2016).

We found 8 bursts across three of the Effelsberg radio telescope observations. The bursts were coherently dedispersed to a DM of 551.92 pc cm[−][3] (Appendix C) with `SFXC`. The resulting files contain the Stokes I (total intensity) data with a time and frequency resolution of 32 µs and 500 kHz, respectively, and were written out as 32-bit filterbank files. The dynamic spectra of these bursts are shown in Figure 1 where they are averaged in time to increase their visibility. For every burst we determine its time of arrival (ToA), temporal width, bandwidth, peak S/N, fluence, spectral luminosity, and peak flux density (see Table 2). We find 18 bursts in 125 observations (∼106 hours; Appendix B). Baseband data were not recorded during the NRT observations and therefore the bursts have a best possible time and frequency resolution of 16 µs and 4 MHz, respectively; and their intrachannel dispersion is corrected for using an MJD-dependent DM-value (Appendix B). We show the bursts and tabulate their properties in Appendix B.

We determine the best structure-maximized DM for burst B3 (Appendix C) and find that it is 551.92 ± 0.33 pc cm[−][3]. We do not determine the DM for the other bursts found with the Effelsberg radio telescope due to their low S/N (Figure 1). For bursts detected with NRT, we determine the DM for the brightest burst of every MJD, which are tabulated in Table B.1. These values, together with previously published DM measurements of FRB 20121102A, are plotted as a function of time in Figure 5. Since FRB 20121102A’s discovery, the DM first increased from ∼560 pc cm[−][3] to ∼566 pc cm[−][3] (late 2019), and then a substantial decrease of ∼25 units was observed in ∼5 years time (DM of ∼ 541 pc cm[−][3] in August 2025). To determine the fractional change in the DM contribution of the host galaxy and the local environment, DMhost+local, of FRB 20121102A we need to subtract the DM contributions of the Milky Way interstellar medium (DMMW,ISM), the Milky Way halo (DMMW,Halo), and the intergalactic medium (DMIGM) from the observed DM. The Galactic electron density model `NE2001p` estimates DMMW,ISM = 188 pc cm[−][3] for the line of sight (LoS) of FRB 20121102A (Ocker & Cordes 2024). However, two new high-DM pulsars near the LoS of FRB 20121102A (l = 174.88[◦], b = −0.22[◦]) were recently discovered; PSR J0557+2442g (l = 184.97[◦], b = +0.08[◦]) is 10[◦] away from FRB 20121102A has a DM of 195 pc cm[−][3] and PSR J0517+3436g (l = 172.04[◦], b = −1.82[◦]) is 3[◦] away and has a DM of 192 pc cm[−][3] (Han et al. 2025). Therefore we assume a DMMW,ISM = 200 pc cm[−][3] for the LoS of FRB 20121102A. We assume DMMW,Halo = 41 pc cm[−][3] (Yamasaki & Totani 2020), which is supported by Cook et al. (2023) constraints placed at higher Galactic latitudes. Finally, we assume DMIGM = 169 pc cm[−][3] (using the relation from Macquart et al. 2020, with z = 0.193). The inferred DM of the host galaxy and the local environment of FRB 20121102A are shown on the right side of Figure 5. We assume that DMhost remains constant and thus show that the fractional change in the DM of the local environment, DMlocal, is ≳15%.

We chose not to correlate the data from the PRECISE/EVN observation PR268A because only one weak burst was found, the observation was short (Table 1), and it was strongly affected by RFI. Observation PR269A was not correlated because no bursts were found. The data of all participating stations from observations PR242A (Epoch 1) and PR243A (Epoch 2) were transferred to the Joint Institute for VLBI ERIC (JIVE, in the Netherlands) for correlation with the FX Software Correlator `SFXC` (Keimpema et al. 2015). The participating stations were: Effelsberg (Ef; Germany), Toru´n (Tr; Poland), Onsala (O8; Sweden), Westerbork RT-1 (Wb; Netherlands), Noto (Nt; Italy), Medicina (Mc; Italy, only participated at Epoch 1), Irbene (Ir; Latvia) and six e-MERLIN stations (Cambridge (Cm), Darnhall (Da), Defford (De), Knockin (Kn), Pickmere (Pi), and Jodrell Bank Mark II (Jm); United Kingdom). The calibrator data and continuum data of FRB 20121102A were correlated using 2-s integrations and 0.5-MHz channel widths (64 channels per 32-MHz subband). The continuum data of FRB 20121102A, as well as the burst data, were referenced to a phase centre of α = 05[h] 31[m] 58.7[s], δ = +33[◦] 08[′] 52.57[′′] (J2000, ICRF), which is ∼30 mas (milliarcseconds) away from the previously published C-band PRS position (α = 05[h] 31[m] 58.70159[s], δ = +33[◦] 08[′] 52.550100[′′] (J2000, ICRF) Marcote et al. 2017). The correlation phase centre and the C-band PRS position are shown in Figure 2. The burst data (A1, A2 and B1–B5) were coherently dedispersed and correlated using various gates that depend on the temporal widths and arrival times of the bursts and are also channelized to 64 channels per subband. All output from the `SFXC` correlator is saved as `FITS-IDI` (Flexible Image Transport System Interferometry Data Interchange) files (e.g., Greisen 2003).

The PRS that is associated with FRB 20121102A is clearly detected and shown in Figures 4 and Appendix D. In Figure 4 contour lines of the PRS flux are drawn, which start at five times the standard deviation of the outer regions of the dirty image (thus excluding the PRS itself) and increase by factors of √2. The flux density of the PRS is S1.4 GHz = 155 ± 26 µJy beam[−][1]. The uncertainty on the flux density is the combination of the standard deviation of the dirty image and a 15% calibration error added in quadrature. Similar to Moroianu et al. (2025), we do not remove the burst windows from the continuum data. Given the fluences of the bursts (Table 2) and the duration of the observations (Appendix A), we estimate that the bursts contribute ≲0.2 µJy to the continuum data. Since this is roughly two orders of magnitude less than the thermal noise in our images, this contribution can be dismissed. We calculate the luminosity as: Assuming a luminosity distance, DL, to FRB 20121102A of 972 Mpc, isotropic emission and z = 0.193 (Tendulkar et al. 2017), this corresponds to a luminosity of L1.4 GHz = (2.09 ± 0.35) × 10[29] erg s[−][1] Hz[−][1]. The fitted position of the PRS, combining both epochs is: The separation between the position of the bursts and the position of the PRS is 3.6 mas, which, given the distance to FRB 20121102A, translates to ∼12 pc. Given that the sizes of the synthesised beams of the burst data and the PRS data are roughly equal, we also estimate the error on the PRS position to be ∼2.5 mas. Therefore, the bursts and the PRS are 1σ consistent with being co-located.

evidence.original_text: We processed and calibrated the correlated continuum data of the calibrators and the PRS associated with FRB 20121102A using `AIPS` (Greisen 2003). Standard EVN calibration tables were used, which contain the parallactic angle correction and a-priori gain correction, using the gain curves and system temperature measurements that the stations recorded during the observations. We also applied the EVN flagging table, ignoring all data from antennas that were still slewing. Additionally, we flagged ∼15% (5 channels on each side) at the edges of the subbands, where antenna sensitivity significantly drops. The `AIPS` task `VLBATECR` was used to correct for the ionospheric dispersive delays (Petrov 2023). We note however that, in this case, omitting the `VLBATECR` step results in no measurable shift in the FRB 20121102A PRS position but increases the flux density (Section 4) by ∼3% (well within the measured uncertainties). We used the `AIPS` tool `spflg` to manually flag the visibilities of the fringe finder, phase calibrator, check source and the continuum data of FRB 20121102A as a function of time, frequency, and baseline. At the start of the calibration the scan on the bright fringe finder was used to correct for the instrumental delay between subbands (i.e., using `fring` without fitting for a rate), with the assumption that that delay remains constant during the observation. Next, the `AIPS` task `fring` was used on all scans of the phase calibrator to determine the group delay and phase rate (i.e., the phase solutions) as a function of time and frequency. Finally, the `AIPS` task `bpass` was used on the fringe finder scan. The calibrated and flagged visibilities of both epochs were concatenated and imaged in `Difmap` (Shepherd 1997) to create a model of the phase calibrator that was used for self calibration. The calibration solutions were transferred and interpolated to the FRB 20121102A continuum data. The flagged, concatenated, and calibrated visibilities were exported as `UVFITS` files with `fittp`, imported in `CASA` with `importuvfits` and imaged with `tclean`.

entries.description: VLBI data correlation and calibration procedure using SFXC, AIPS, and CASA

evidence.original_text: The burst visibilities were calibrated differently. We started by importing all `FITS-IDI` files of the bursts and calibrator data in `CASA` using `importfitsidi`. Next, we used `split` to create a `CASA` measurement set that only contains the visibilities of the scans of the fringe finder, the bursts, and the 13 phase calibrator scans that bracket the bursts. This results in a much smaller dataset that allows for quicker processing and manual RFI flagging. Similar to the continuum data that was processed with `AIPS`, we flagged ∼15% of the subband edges and we corrected for the ionosphere with `CASA`’s `gencal` and `tec_maps` (i.e., the `AIPS` equivalent of `VLBATECR`). We manually flagged all time ranges for which a particular antenna was still slewing and flagged for RFI in the fringe finder scans as a function of time, frequency and baseline. We did the same for each phase calibrator bracket, keeping track of which frequency channels, as a function of baseline, were flagged. For the individual bursts in such a bracket, we flagged the same frequency channels. This approach was chosen because each burst has only one time integration, making it difficult to distinguish between strong scintillation, narrowband RFI and instrumental effects. We used `CASA`’s `fringefit`, without fitting for fringe rates, on the fringe finder scans to correct for the instrumental delay between subbands and on the phase calibrator scans to determine the phase, delay, and delay rate as a function of time and frequency. Finally, `CASA`’s `bandpass` was used to determine the antenna response, for both phase and amplitude, as a function of frequency. The calibration solutions were transferred to the flagged burst visibilities. Similar to the continuum data, we made use of `tclean` to create dirty maps (i.e., the inverse Fourier transform of the visibilities), of individual bursts and combinations of bursts. All images, of the burst(s) and the corresponding PRS, were made with a cellsize of 1 mas and Briggs weighting with a robustness parameter of 0.5.

evidence.original_text: In this work, we used the EVN to strongly constrain the offset between the FRB 20121102A burst source and its associated PRS, demonstrating that they are co-located to within ≲4 mas, which is equivalent to ≲12 pc transverse offset in the host galaxy. This analysis significantly improves on the previous work of Marcote et al. (2017), where limited (u, v)-coverage led to a 4× larger uncertainty in the position of the FRB 20121102A burst source. In addition, we find that our PRS flux density measurements are consistent with other observations, taken at different epochs and with varying spatial resolution (e.g., Rhodes et al. 2023); and, we map the time-variable DM of FRB 20121102A, combining our data with measurements from many other telescopes (Spitler et al. 2014, 2016; Hessels et al. 2019; Snelders et al. 2023; Oostrum et al. 2020; Platts et al. 2021; Majid et al. 2020; Li et al. 2021; Wang et al. 2025b; Zhang et al. 2025).

FRB 20121102A is the original repeating fast radio burst (FRB) source and also the first to be localised to milliarcsecond precision using very-long-baseline interferometry (VLBI). It has been active for over 13 years and resides in an extreme magneto-ionic environment in a dwarf host galaxy at a distance of ∼1 Gpc. In this work, we use the European VLBI Network (EVN) to (re-)localise FRB 20121102A and its associated persistent radio source (PRS). We confirm that the two are co-located — improving on previous results by a factor of ∼4 and constraining the FRB and PRS co-location to ∼12 pc transverse offset. Over a decade, the PRS luminosity on milliarcsecond scales remains consistent with measurements on larger angular scales, showing that the PRS is still compact. We also present the detection of 18 bursts with the Nançay Radio Telescope (NRT) as part of our ÉCLAT monitoring program. These bursts, together with previously published results, show that the observed dispersion measure (DM) of FRB 20121102A has dropped by ∼25 pc cm[−][3] in the past five years, highlighting a fractional decrease in the local DM contribution of ≳15 %. We discuss potential physical scenarios and highlight possible future observations that will help reveal the nature of FRB 20121102A, which is one of only a few known FRBs with a luminous PRS.

The separation between the position of the bursts and the position of the PRS is 3.6 mas, which, given the distance to FRB 20121102A, translates to ∼12 pc. Given that the sizes of the synthesised beams of the burst data and the PRS data are roughly equal, we also estimate the error on the PRS position to be ∼2.5 mas. Therefore, the bursts and the PRS are 1σ consistent with being co-located. To compare these positions with the previously determined C-band (∼4.5 GHz) PRS detection (Marcote et al. 2017), updates to the Radio Fundamental Catalog (RFC; Petrov & Kovalev 2025) need to be taken into account. In both this work, as in Marcote et al. (2017), the source J0529+3209 was used as the phase calibrator. The assumed position of this source has shifted by +0.3 mas in right ascension and by +1.4 mas in declination between the RFC versions of 2016 (Marcote et al. 2017) and 2022 (this work). Thus, to make the comparison between 2016 and 2022 correct, we shift the C-band PRS position with the same increase in right ascension and declination. In all applicable figures, the updated C-band PRS position is shown as a solid solid cyan ‘+’. In Figure 3 we also show the C-band PRS position if updates to the RFC are not taken into account.

We determine the best structure-maximized DM for burst B3 (Appendix C) and find that it is 551.92 ± 0.33 pc cm[−][3] . We do not determine the DM for the other bursts found with the Effelsberg radio telescope due to their low S/N (Figure 1). For bursts detected with NRT, we determine the DM for the brightest burst of every MJD, which are tabulated in Table B.1. These values, together with previously published DM measurements of FRB 20121102A, are plotted as a function of time in Figure 5. Since FRB 20121102A’s discovery, the DM first increased from ∼560 pc cm[−][3] to ∼566 pc cm[−][3] (late 2019), and then a substantial decrease of ∼25 units was observed in ∼5 years time (DM of ∼ 541 pc cm[−][3] in August 2025). (2023) constraints placed at higher Galactic latitudes. Finally, we assume DMIGM = 169 pc cm[−][3] (using the relation from Macquart et al. 2020, with _z_ = 0.193). The inferred DM of the host galaxy and the local environment of FRB 20121102A are shown on the right side of Figure 5. We do not find any significant linear or circular polarisation in our brightest burst, B3. Circular polarisation in bursts from FRB 20121102A occur in less than 1 % of the bursts (Feng et al. 2022) and it is known that the linear polarisation fraction decreases with decreasing frequency (e.g., Plavin et al. 2022). Based on Wang et al. (2025b) we suspect that at our observing frequencies (∼1.4 GHz) the linear polarisation fraction is ∼5 % and that we do not have enough S/N in B3 to measure any significant linear polarisation. We do not investigate the NRT data for any polarimetric properties due to the large intra-channel depolarisation (Appendix E) and the other Effelsberg-detected bursts are not investigated due to their low S/N.

While we cannot strictly rule out that FRB 20121102A and its coincident PRS are only indirectly associated, the existence of now several FRB-PRS systems (Moroianu et al. 2025), in dwarf galaxies, with high and variable local DM/RM, suggests they could be a distinct type of FRB source and not just a matter of circumstance. We identify the following as the most promising ways to clarify the nature of FRB 20121102A and its PRS: - Global VLBI observations at frequencies ≳8 GHz (X-band) will place even stronger constraints (expected to be ∼2 times better than Marcote et al. 2017) on the size of the PRS, and may even begin to resolve it. If the PRS is resolved, these observations will also tell how fast it is expanding. A direct measurement of the PRS size could argue for the accreting binary scenario. - Likewise, high-sensitivity, global VLBI observations at ≳4 GHz, that detect multiple bursts from FRB 20121102A could place ∼3–10 × stronger constraints on the co-location with the PRS. Any measurable separation would argue for the indirect association scenario. Furthermore, these measurements will place constraints on the burst source’s kick velocity if the PRS is an SNR. - If the luminosity of the PRS is slowly decreasing — e.g., due to rotational slowdown or decreased accretion rate — it might take a couple decades before this can be robustly disentangled from the variability of the PRS due to interstellar scintillation. For context, the flux density of the Cassiopeia A SNR, estimated to be about 350 years old, decreases by ∼0.7 % per year (see, e.g., Trotter et al. 2017). - Currently the lowest-frequency detection of the PRS is at 400 MHz (Resmi et al. 2021). The spectral energy distribution (SED) of the PRS is nearly flat between 400 GHz and 10 GHz (see, e.g., Figure 2 of Resmi et al. 2021). Since FRB 20121102A is located in the direction of the Galactic plane ( _l_ = 174.88[◦] , _b_ = −0.22[◦] ), interferometric observations at low frequencies are challenging. Nonetheless, observations at ∼150 MHz with, e.g., LOFAR would be useful to see if the PRS is affected by self-absorption. Likewise, it would be interesting to see if, and how, the shape of the SED changes over time, since the spectral index is not expected to be constant over the course of a MWN or hypernebula’s lifetime. - Lastly, we encourage continued monitoring of FRB 20121102A, also outside of its expected activity window, to check for DM and RM evolution and periodic activity. A prolonged DM decrease towards FRB 20121102A could be explained in an expanding SNR scenario. Likewise, a sign flip in the RM (Anna-Thomas et al. 2023) of FRB 20121102A could suggest that the system is in a binary system. Also, changes in the scattering and/or scintillation properties of the bursts would be interesting, since they will largely track changes in the local environment.
